messages.content: Three survivors attended Home Health Aid Programs; 1-Survisor attending EKG Program; 1 Survivor attend ELS program; 1 Survivor attended Medical assistance program; and 2 survivors were attending college level course.

In total, there were 3 survivors attending Home Health Aid Programs, 1 survivor attending an EKG Program, 1 survivor attending an ELS program, 1 survivor attending a Medical Assistance program, and 2 survivors attending college level courses.

During this reporting period, in total, there are 8 women participating in the vocational training program: Three women are enrolled in Home Health Aid Programs 
One attending an EKG Program technician training course 
One enrolled in an ELS program
One attending a Medical Assistance program
Two attending college level courses

During this reporting period, there are a total of 8 women participating in the vocational training program. Out of these, three women are enrolled in Home Health Aid Programs, one is attending an EKG Program technician training course, one is enrolled in an ELS program, one is attending a Medical Assistance program, and two are attending college level courses.

These courses help clients gain specialized knowledge and practical skills in fields such as healthcare, cosmetology, technology, hospitality, among other trades. By obtaining these certificates, survivors can pursue secure employment opportunities with higher earning potential, helping survivors rebuild their lives, break the cycle of violence, and create a brighter future for themselves and their families.

These vocational training programs provide crucial opportunities for survivors to gain specialized knowledge and practical skills in various fields such as healthcare, cosmetology, technology, hospitality, and other trades. By obtaining these certificates, survivors are empowered to pursue secure employment opportunities with higher earning potential, enabling them to rebuild their lives and break the cycle of violence. This vocational training opens up doors for survivors to create a brighter future for themselves and their families.

Transitioning survivors of domestic violence out of shelters into safe and independent living is a key aspect of the work we do at Genesis. The Wolk Family funded vocational training program plays a vital role in this transition by providing survivors with the necessary tools, support, and skills needed to secure stable employment that leads to long-term financial independence. Through this program, survivors are supported in completing focused certificate-based job courses, helping them gain specialized knowledge and practical skills in fields such as healthcare, cosmetology, technology, hospitality, among other trades. By obtaining these certificates, survivors are empowered to pursue secure employment opportunities with higher earning potential, enabling them to rebuild their lives and break the cycle of abuse.

Transitioning survivors of domestic violence out of shelters into safe and independent living is a crucial focus of our work at Genesis. The Wolk Family-funded vocational training program plays a vital role in this transition process by equipping survivors with the necessary tools, support, and skills to secure stable employment and achieve long-term financial independence. Through this program, survivors receive comprehensive assistance in completing certificate-based job courses that offer specialized knowledge and practical skills in various fields, including healthcare, cosmetology, technology, hospitality, and more. By obtaining these certificates, survivors are empowered to pursue secure employment opportunities with higher earning potential, enabling them to rebuild their lives and break free from the cycle of abuse.
Grant funding from the Helen Wolk Family Foundation supports three projects at Genesis this year. The projects and their respective accomplishments include:

Grant funding from the Helen Wolk Family Foundation has supported three projects at Genesis this year. Here are the projects and their respective accomplishments:

1. Vocational Training Program: This program has provided survivors with specialized training and certifications in fields such as healthcare, cosmetology, technology, and hospitality. Thanks to the funding, survivors have successfully completed these courses and gained essential skills for secure employment.

2. Transitional Housing Program: The grant has enabled Genesis to provide safe and supportive transitional housing for survivors as they transition out of shelters. This program has helped survivors establish independent living arrangements and create stable environments for themselves and their families.

3. Counseling and Support Services: The funding has supported counseling and support services for survivors, ensuring they have access to critical mental health resources. This program has helped survivors heal from trauma, develop coping mechanisms, and regain control of their lives.

The support from the Helen Wolk Family Foundation has been instrumental in these accomplishments, enabling Genesis to provide comprehensive support to survivors of domestic violence and empower them along their journey towards safety and independence.

During the first half of the grant period, we had a total of 10 women participating in the program with three completing a certificate-based courses and secured employment as security officers and home health aides.

During the first half of the grant period, we had a total of 10 women participating in the program. Out of these, three women successfully completed certificate-based courses and were able to secure employment as security officers and home health aides. This is a significant accomplishment as it demonstrates the effectiveness of the vocational training program in equipping survivors with essential skills and helping them secure stable employment opportunities. We are proud of these women for their dedication and resilience in rebuilding their lives and congratulate them on their accomplishments.

During this reporting period, there are a total of 8 women participating in the vocational training program. Of these, three women are enrolled in Home Health Aid Programs, one is taking an EKG technician training course, one is enrolled in an ELS program, one is attending a Medical Assistance program, and two are taking college courses at Lehman College to further their education positioning themself for gainful employment. Further, we expect the three women enrolled in the Home Health Aid Program to complete their course training in December 2023 and receive their CNAs certification.

During this reporting period, there are 8 women participating in the vocational training program. Among them, three women are currently enrolled in Home Health Aid Programs, one is pursuing an EKG technician training course, one is enrolled in an ELS program, one is attending a Medical Assistance program, and two are taking college courses at Lehman College to enhance their education and job prospects. Additionally, we anticipate the three women enrolled in the Home Health Aid Program to complete their training in December 2023 and receive their Certified Nursing Assistant (CNA) certification. These achievements are significant steps towards their goal of securing gainful employment and ultimately achieving financial independence.
The Tutoring, Empowerment and Recreational Program continues to be beneficial in helping children adjust to shelter life, away from their old homes, schools, family, and friends.

The Tutoring, Empowerment, and Recreational Program remains a valuable resource in assisting children with adjusting to shelter life, especially as they navigate the challenges of being separated from their previous homes, schools, family, and friends. This program provides essential support, guidance, and activities to help children cope with these significant life changes. By offering tutoring services, children can continue their education and maintain a sense of normalcy amidst upheaval. Additionally, empowerment activities empower children to build resilience, self-esteem, and confidence, helping them navigate their current circumstances and prepare for a brighter future. The recreational component allows children to engage in fun, age-appropriate activities, fostering a sense of joy and creating positive experiences during this challenging time. The program's holistic approach addresses the unique needs of each child, promoting their emotional well-being and assisting them in adapting to their new environment.

This program provides essential support to families including targeted activities to help children cope with this significant life change. We offering tutoring and homework help, so children can get the help needed to continue their education and maintain a sense of normalcy amidst upheaval.

The Tutoring, Empowerment, and Recreational Program play a crucial role in providing vital support to families during this significant life change. One of the key components of the program is offering tutoring and homework help to children, ensuring they receive the assistance they need to continue their education and maintain a sense of normalcy despite the challenges they face. By providing targeted activities and resources, the program aims to support children in their academic development and help them adapt to their new circumstances. This education-focused support is essential in helping children cope with the changes they are experiencing and providing them with the tools to thrive academically. By offering tutoring and homework help, the program aims to minimize the disruption to their education and provide a stable and supportive learning environment.

The children continue to utilize the Wolk donated computer lab daily for tutoring and homework help, to build computer skills and to enjoy educational games.  The children are also supported in activities that support their fine and gross motor skills to help them strengthen their social and emotional and academic skills. They continue to participate in arts and crafts projects, are led in literacy read-alouds with developmentally appropriate texts and participate in activity based group discussions. We also host volunteer led reading groups on Fridays with interactive activities that encourage independent reading and read-alouds and incorporate arts and craft projects based on books read.

The children have been actively engaging with the Wolk donated computer lab on a daily basis, utilizing it for tutoring, homework help, and to enhance their computer skills. They also enjoy educational games that contribute to their overall learning experience. Additionally, the program recognizes the importance of supporting the children's fine and gross motor skills, as these skills are crucial for their social, emotional, and academic development. Various activities are designed to help children strengthen these skills, including arts and crafts projects. 

Literacy read-alouds are incorporated into the program, using developmentally appropriate texts that foster a love for reading and enhance their literacy skills. Activity-based group discussions further encourage engagement and critical thinking. 

On Fridays, volunteer-led reading groups are organized, providing interactive activities that encourage independent reading and read-alouds. These sessions are complemented with related arts and crafts projects based on the books that were read. This multi-faceted approach ensures that children have a well-rounded and engaging learning experience, supporting their cognitive, social, and emotional growth.
We employ activities designed to help children strengthen these skills, including various arts and crafts projects, and outings to museums and the Bronx zoo.

To support the development of children's fine and gross motor skills, our program incorporates various activities, including engaging arts and crafts projects. These projects not only provide an opportunity for creativity but also help children improve their coordination and dexterity.

In addition to arts and crafts, we organize special outings to museums and the Bronx Zoo. These outings provide children with valuable opportunities to explore the world outside the shelter, learn about different subjects, and engage in interactive educational experiences. Visiting museums allows children to appreciate art, culture, and history, while trips to the zoo offer them a chance to learn about animals and their habitats.

These outings not only provide a change of scenery but also contribute to the children's overall learning and development. By exposing them to new environments and educational experiences, we aim to broaden their horizons and foster a love for learning beyond the shelter setting.

Ms. V came to our facility on 6/24/23. Ms. V was able to provide descriptive details of her current domestic violence incident, as well as her personal take on what appears to be an ongoing domestic violence relationship. Client shared that the most recent abuse occurred on 4/25/23, while they were placed in a family shelter in Queens. She stated that the father of her children came home and wanted to ask for forgiveness for his behavior and mal-treatment. When she told him that she wanted to end the relationship he took out a knife and threatened to kill her if she left him, stating that if “she isn’t with him she will not be with anyone”. Ms. V feared for her safety and then told him that she will stay and that she would not contact the police.  Shortly after she was threatened, she was able to notify the shelter staff of the incident and the police were called. He was arrested and an Order of protection was issued. Initially upon arrival the focus of Ms. V was to address the trauma associated with her DV relationship. SW was able to build a working relationship with Ms. V through weekly sessions as well as domestic violence support groups for parenting/DV survivors. Since Ms. V does not speak English her information was reviewed with her in Spanish and she would provide her insight and any questions during her weekly sessions. Shortly after staying with us she began to have problems with her teenage daughter, who became rebellious and confrontational. The majority of the work has been focusing on family therapy and improving the relationship between Mom and her oldest daughter R. At this point the most pressing need for this mother and her children is to secure permanent housing. DV counseling and support are the other concerns for the family. SW provided client with a referral for the Family Justice Center, in the Bronx.

Ms. V arrived at our facility on 6/24/23 and shared detailed information about her current domestic violence situation and her perspective on an ongoing abusive relationship. She described a recent incident on 4/25/23, while they were placed in a family shelter in Queens. Ms. V recounted that her children's father came home and apologized for his previous abusive behavior. However, when she expressed her desire to end the relationship, he became violent and threatened her with a knife, stating that if she left him, no one else would have her. Fearing for her safety, Ms. V decided to stay and not contact the police. Shortly after the incident, she was able to inform shelter staff, who promptly called the police. He was subsequently arrested, and an Order of Protection was issued.

Upon arrival, Ms. V's primary focus was addressing the trauma associated with her domestic violence relationship. Our social worker (SW) established a solid working relationship with Ms. V through weekly sessions and by involving her in domestic violence support groups for parenting and DV survivors. Considering that Ms. V doesn't speak English fluently, her information was reviewed and discussed in Spanish during these sessions to ensure comprehension and promote effective communication.

Not long after staying with us, Ms. V began experiencing difficulties with her teenage daughter, who exhibited rebellious and confrontational behavior. Much of the therapeutic work has involved family therapy and improving the relationship between Ms. V and her oldest daughter, referred to as R.

Currently, the most urgent need for this family is to secure permanent housing. Additionally, Ms. V and her children require ongoing DV counseling and support services. To address these needs, the SW provided Ms. V with a referral to the Family Justice Center in the Bronx, where she can access comprehensive assistance and resources specific to domestic violence cases.

The most pressing concern over the past few weeks has been the relationship between Ms. V and her oldest daughter R.  R has been staying out past her curfew and not returning to the shelter immediately after school as expected, sometimes arriving at the shelter in the late evening after 8-9pm. Ms. V has had to contact the NYPD on multiple occasions as R has stayed out on two at least 4 occasions. On two of those occasions Ms. V was able to file a missing person report with the NYPD and they were able to assist in locating/contacting R and ensuring she returns to the shelter. However on the other two occasions the NYPD stated they were unable to consider R missing as she made contact with her mother and even went to school despite not making contact with her family for a period that was close to 20 hours. SW suggested client contact the Person In Need of Supervision (PINS) division to provide assistance as things did not seem to be improving; however ACS recently was notified of the familial situation and has begun working with the family to determine next steps. R has shared with ACS that her desire is to reside with her biological father moving forward and it appears as though ACS is currently exploring this as a viable option. SW is also recommending that the family continue to participate in therapy as well as explore preventative services to minimize further trauma to the family.

In recent weeks, the relationship between Ms. V and her oldest daughter, R, has become a significant concern. R has been consistently staying out past her curfew and not returning to the shelter immediately after school, sometimes arriving as late as 8-9pm. As a result, Ms. V has had to contact the NYPD on multiple occasions, with two instances resulting in filing missing person reports to locate and contact R to ensure her safe return to the shelter.

However, on the other two occasions, the NYPD stated that they could not consider R as missing, as she had contacted her mother and even attended school during the period she was not in contact with her family, although it extended close to 20 hours. Recognizing the ongoing challenges, the social worker (SW) suggested that Ms. V consider reaching out to the Person In Need of Supervision (PINS) division for assistance. However, it is important to note that The Administration for Children's Services (ACS) has recently been informed about the family's situation and has begun working with them to determine the appropriate next steps.

During discussions with ACS, R has expressed her desire to reside with her biological father going forward. It appears that ACS is currently exploring this possibility as a viable option for R's living arrangement. In the meantime, the SW continues to recommend that the family actively engage in therapy to address the underlying concerns and work towards resolving the strained relationship. Additionally, the SW suggests exploring preventative services to minimize the potential for further trauma within the family dynamics. It is crucial to ensure ongoing support and interventions to promote the well-being of both Ms. V and R during this challenging time.

•         Client was recently enrolled in a home health aide training course at the ABC school. Client shared that she is excited about completing her training and looking forward to beginning work and securing a better life for herself and her 3 children.
•         Ms. V recently completed her training course as a home health aide. Client received her course completion certificate on 10/3/23. Client is excited to finally have received her certificate of completion. Client has now turned her attention towards finding a job and wants to start working as soon as possible so she can begin to save money towards moving into a home for herself and her children.
•         Client was recently able to open a Public Assistance case with her children added to her budget. This took some time as there was an ongoing investigation into the case, as the biological father had all of the children active on his case. It took several visits to the PA office and several residency letters being provided to rectify the situation, however, client claims the situation has been rectified.

• Ms. V has recently enrolled in a home health aide training course at the ABC school. She shared her excitement about completing the training and her eagerness to begin working, as this will allow her to secure a better life for herself and her three children. The vocational training program has been instrumental in providing her with the necessary skills and certification to pursue a career in the healthcare field. This achievement brings her one step closer to achieving her goals.

• As of 10/3/23, Ms. V has successfully completed her training course as a home health aide and received her course completion certificate. This is a significant milestone for her, and she is thrilled about this accomplishment. With her certification in hand, her focus now shifts towards finding employment so that she can start working as soon as possible. Her priority is to begin earning an income and saving money for her ultimate goal of securing a suitable home for herself and her children.
